title: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage
exclusions: Does not evaluate assessment thoroughness, confirm that supplier relationships satisfy Implementing Regulation criteria, determine Art.22 critical supply chain status, or replace procurement review. Only tracks assessment coverage breadth and Dependabot alert volumes.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.25` output, Dependabot reconciliation worksheet, and signed lookup version hash stored with the NIS2 supplier risk evidence bundle.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(d) requires supply chain security. The UC tracks assessment coverage breadth across the supplier register and surfaces Dependabot alerts on supplier code dependencies. It cannot evaluate whether the assessments themselves were thorough or that supplier relationships satisfy the Implementing Regulation's proportionality criteria, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.25: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.25' running on nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv, index=github (Dependabot alerts), index=itsm, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Populate the lookup with a tier-1 supplier whose `next_assessment_date` is seven days in the past and ensure the GitHub subsearch returns twelve open `dependabot_alert` events; the combined row appears with `overdue=1` and `open_supply_chain_alerts` above the threshold.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Use the same supplier row but refresh `next_assessment_date` to ninety days ahead and reduce Dependabot fixtures below three open alerts; the scheduled search produces zero rows and the audit log records a clean run.

dataSources: `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` lookup (supplier_id, supplier_name, tier, next_assessment_date, supplier_code_repo); `index=github` `sourcetype=dependabot_alert` (alert_number, repo, severity); `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:task` for linked remediation tasks when Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) pulls `/now/table/sn_task`.
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)), Splunk Add-on for GitHub ([Splunkbase 5596](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5596)) with REST or webhook ingestion for Dependabot.
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv
| eval assessment_due=strptime(next_assessment_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval overdue=if(now()>assessment_due,1,0)
| join supplier_code_repo type=left [
    search index=github sourcetype="dependabot_alert" earliest=-30d
    | stats dc(alert_number) as open_supply_chain_alerts by repo
    | rename repo as supplier_code_repo
]
| fillnull value=0 open_supply_chain_alerts
| where overdue=1 OR open_supply_chain_alerts > 10
| table supplier_name, tier, next_assessment_date, overdue, open_supply_chain_alerts
description: Combines the authoritative supplier assessment register with open Dependabot-style repository alerts so procurement sees both paperwork gaps and concrete open dependency findings per supplier code base.
value: Leadership receives one numbered list that pairs "assessment overdue" with "dependency alerts still open" so board updates do not claim green third-party posture while GitHub still shows dozens of unfixed issues.
implementation:
(1) Publish `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` under `lookups/` with `supplier_id` matching your CMDB key. (2) Map `supplier_code_repo` to GitHub `repo` names (`org/name`). (3) Configure GitHub TA (5596) modular input or HEC for Dependabot JSON. (4) Optional: mirror ServiceNow tasks (1928) to prove compensating documentation exists. (5) Tune threshold `open_supply_chain_alerts > 10` per tier.

**Note:** Normalize `supplier_code_repo` to lowercase `org/repo` to match GitHub `repo` before scheduling.
visualization: Table (supplier_name, tier, overdue flag, alert count), bar chart by tier, single value overdue tier-1 count.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Assessment due date mis-keyed in CSV** — Distinguish: typo pushes next_assessment_date into past. Suppress: governance process to fix lookup; interim `where supplier_name!="EXAMPLE_VENDOR"` filter.
• **Dependabot duplicates same CVE across alerts** — Distinguish: `dc(alert_number)` still inflates counts. Suppress: replace with `dc(security_advisory_id)` after field extract.
• **Supplier uses GitLab not GitHub** — Distinguish: repo field empty, join misses. Suppress: add parallel subsearch for `sourcetype=gitlab:vulnerability` or mark supplier `sca_platform=github` in register.
• **Planned acceptance of low-severity dependency noise** — Distinguish: risk acceptance memo signed for dev-only repos. Suppress: `vuln_exception_register.csv` keyed on repo with `max_cvss`.
• **Quarterly questionnaire cadence mis-aligned with CSV monthly updates** — Distinguish: real-world review happened but CSV stale <30 days. Suppress: sync ServiceNow field `u_next_assessment` nightly via scripted export.
equipmentModels: GitHub Advanced Security (Dependabot alerts, code scanning, secret scanning)
GitLab Ultimate (optional — dependency scanning via sourcetype gitlab:vulnerability)
Splunk Add-on for GitHub (Splunkbase 5596, Dependabot alert ingestion via REST or webhook)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, task/incident tracking for remediation evidence)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `github` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(d)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.25: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `github` under `dependabot_alert`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


7 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Publish `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` under `lookups/` with `supplier_id` matching your CMDB key. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 2:** Map `supplier_code_repo` to GitHub `repo` names (`org/name`). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Configure GitHub TA. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** modular input or HEC for Dependabot JSON. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Optional: mirror ServiceNow tasks. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 6:** to prove compensating documentation exists. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 7:** Tune threshold `open_supply_chain_alerts > 10` per tier.

**Note:** Normalize `supplier_code_repo` to lowercase `org/repo` to match GitHub `repo` before scheduling. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage," the aggregation measures control coverage — the output rows enumerate what IS covered, and an auditor's follow-on question ('what about the rest?') is answered by the gap between this result and the authoritative population.

This search begins with `| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup nis2_supplier_assessment_register.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true nis2_supplier_assessment_register_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 30 days) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.25: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Assessment Coverage]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.25,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `assessment_due`, `next_assessment_date`, `open_supply_chain_alerts`, `overdue`, `supplier_code_repo`, `supplier_name`, `tier`. Run:

```spl
index=github sourcetype=dependabot_alert earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table assessment_due, next_assessment_date, open_supply_chain_alerts, overdue, supplier_code_repo, supplier_name, tier
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `open_supply_chain_alerts`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(open_supply_chain_alerts) as p50 perc90(open_supply_chain_alerts) as p90 perc99(open_supply_chain_alerts) as p99 max(open_supply_chain_alerts) as hi
```

The current threshold `10` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.25: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`supplier_name`, `tier`, `overdue flag`, `alert count`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart By Tier (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value Overdue Tier-1 Count (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We line up the paperwork deadlines for checking outside suppliers with the real open problems reported in the code libraries those suppliers help maintain, so nobody thinks the homework is done when the toolbox is still messy.
